evals.prompt: I have a support chatbot that answers questions from our help docs. Sometimes it makes up features that don't exist or gives wrong pricing info. I need to add a check that verifies the answer is actually grounded in the source docs before showing it to the user. Using GPT-4o-mini.
evals.expected_output: A DSPy module with a verification step that checks if the generated answer is supported by the retrieved context, wrapped with dspy.Refine using a faithfulness reward function.

assertions.name: uses_refine_or_bestn
assertions.description: Uses dspy.Refine or dspy.BestOfN (not deprecated dspy.Assert) for output validation

assertions.name: has_verification_signature
assertions.description: Defines a separate dspy.Signature for verifying answer against context/sources

assertions.name: has_reward_function
assertions.description: Defines a reward function with (args, pred) signature that scores faithfulness

assertions.name: inside_module
assertions.description: Verification logic is inside a dspy.Module or used as a reward function for Refine/BestOfN

assertions.name: grounds_in_context
assertions.description: The verification step checks that claims in the answer are present in the retrieved passages

evals.prompt: We extract invoice data from emails — vendor name, amount, date, line items. The LLM sometimes returns amounts that don't add up (line items don't sum to total), dates in the wrong format, or completely hallucinated vendor names. I need format and consistency checks on the extracted fields.
evals.expected_output: A DSPy module that extracts invoice fields then validates them with a reward function checking amount arithmetic, date format, and non-empty fields, wrapped with dspy.Refine.

assertions.name: uses_pydantic_model
assertions.description: Defines a Pydantic BaseModel for invoice fields with type constraints (Field with ge/le, min_length, etc.)

assertions.name: validates_arithmetic
assertions.description: Reward function checks that line item amounts sum to the total amount

assertions.name: validates_date_format
assertions.description: Uses regex or parsing to validate date format in the reward function

assertions.name: uses_refine_or_bestn
assertions.description: Uses dspy.Refine or dspy.BestOfN with a reward function for validation

assertions.name: has_reward_function
assertions.description: Reward function includes specific details about what failed (e.g., 'Line items sum to $X but total is $Y')

evals.prompt: I'm building a content moderation API and I need a safety filter that blocks responses containing PII (emails, phone numbers, SSNs), hate speech, or prompt injection attempts. It's public-facing so the cost of a bad output is high. What's the most robust approach?
evals.expected_output: A multi-layered checking approach using dspy.Refine with a safety reward function combining regex for PII, an AI safety judge, and BestOfN for high-stakes selection.

assertions.name: uses_regex_for_pii
assertions.description: Includes regex patterns to detect PII (email, phone, SSN formats) in the reward function

assertions.name: uses_safety_judge
assertions.description: Defines a safety-check Signature that uses AI to evaluate content policy compliance

assertions.name: graduated_reward
assertions.description: Uses graduated scoring with heavy penalties for PII/safety and lighter penalties for borderline cases

assertions.name: inside_module_or_reward
assertions.description: All checks are inside a dspy.Module or used as reward functions for Refine/BestOfN

assertions.name: handles_high_stakes
assertions.description: Uses Refine, BestOfN, or multi-layer approach appropriate for public-facing high-stakes output
